name: Binomial Theorem — Expanding (a + b)ⁿ
whatIsIt: The binomial theorem expands (a + b)ⁿ without multiplying it out by hand. The coefficients are the combinations nC0, nC1, …, nCn — exactly the rows of PASCAL'S TRIANGLE. For example (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² and (a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³. An expansion of (a + b)ⁿ has n + 1 terms, and the powers of a fall while the powers of b rise.
whyNeeded: Expanding powers of sums appears everywhere: probability (coin tosses), approximations, compound growth, and algebra proofs. Pascal's triangle gives the coefficients instantly instead of tedious multiplication.
realLifeUses: Probabilities of heads/tails patterns in repeated coin tosses
Expanding algebra in physics and engineering formulas

vocabulary.meaning: An expression with two terms, like a + b.
A triangle of numbers where each entry is the sum of the two above it; its rows are the binomial coefficients.
The number multiplying each term in the expansion of (a + b)ⁿ.
An expansion of (a + b)ⁿ has n + 1 terms.

story.title: Pascal's Staircase
story.text: Didi draws a triangle: 1; then 1 1; then 1 2 1; then 1 3 3 1 — each number the sum of the two above. 'These are the coefficients of (a + b)ⁿ,' she says. For (a + b)² Rehan reads off 1, 2, 1 and writes a² + 2ab + b². 'And (a + b)² has 2 + 1 = 3 terms,' he notes, spotting the n + 1 rule.
story.extractedProblem: Using Pascal's triangle row 1 2 1, expand (a + b)².

props.label: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² as a square of side (a + b)

visual.caption: A square of side (a + b) splits into a², two ab rectangles, and b² — that's why the middle coefficient is 2.

teachingGallery.title: Pascal gives the coefficients
teachingGallery.note: Each row of Pascal's triangle is the list of coefficients for that power.

examples.caption: The square picture shows the a², 2ab, b² pieces.

standardMethod.summary: Read the coefficients from Pascal's triangle (row n), then attach falling powers of a and rising powers of b. The expansion has n + 1 terms.
steps: Write Pascal's triangle down to row n (each entry = sum of the two above).
The row gives the coefficients nC0, nC1, …, nCn.
Attach powers: a starts at n and falls to 0; b starts at 0 and rises to n.
Combine: term r is nCr · a^(n−r) · b^r.
Check: the number of terms is n + 1, and coefficients read the same forwards and backwards.

workedExamples.problem: How many terms are in the expansion of (a + b)⁵?

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing (a + b)² = a² + b² and forgetting the 2ab middle term.
commonMistakes.fix: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b². The middle coefficient is 2 (from Pascal's 1 2 1) — never drop it.
It has n + 1 terms — the powers of b run from 0 up to n, which is n + 1 values.

easy.q: In (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b², what is the middle coefficient?
How many terms are in the expansion of (a + b)³?

medium.q: In (a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³, the coefficient of a²b is?

challenge.q: Sum of all coefficients of (a + b)⁴ equals 2⁴ (put a = b = 1). That is?
A student writes (a + b)² = a² + b². What's the fix?

options.label: Add the middle term: a² + 2ab + b²

challenge.answer: Add the middle term: a² + 2ab + b²

questions.q: The coefficients of (a + b)ⁿ come from…

teachBackPrompt: Draw Pascal's triangle down to 1 3 3 1 for a grown-up and use it to expand (a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³. Explain that (a + b)ⁿ has n + 1 terms and never forget the middle 2ab in (a + b)².

revisionCard.summary: (a + b)ⁿ expands with coefficients from Pascal's triangle (row n = nC0…nCn). Powers of a fall, powers of b rise. It has n + 1 terms. Remember (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² and (a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³. Sum of coefficients = 2ⁿ.

rememberIt.unpack: General term T(r+1) = nCr a^(n-r) b^r. Powers of a go down, b go up, always adding to n. There are n+1 terms.

studentQuestions.q: How do I find one term without expanding everything?
studentQuestions.a: Use T(r+1) = nCr a^(n-r) b^r. Pick the r that gives the power you want.

studentQuestions.q: Where do the coefficients come from?
studentQuestions.a: The nCr values - the same numbers as the rows of Pascal's triangle.

formulas.name: General term
formulas.remember: Powers of a and b always add to n
formulas.whenToUse: Find a specific term or coefficient.
